title: Labour’s hardest war is with the electorate
gist: Political scientist Ben Ansell argues Keir Starmer's Labour government pursued a 'reverse Schlieffen plan' — trying to fight the left (Greens/Lib Dems) and right (Reform UK) simultaneously by tacking rightward on immigration while losing urban left seats — and left incoming PM Andy Burnham a roughly £5bn fiscal hole in defense spending plus a party stuck near 20 per cent in the polls.

insights.claim: Following its 2024 election victory, Labour under Starmer and Morgan McSweeney pursued what the author calls a 'reverse Schlieffen plan' — tacking right on immigration to court Reform UK voters while simultaneously trying to hold its left flank — and ended up under attack from both directions at once, ceding urban seats to the Greens and Liberal Democrats while failing to win over Reform-inclined voters.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: A structural diagnosis of why a governing party's polling has collapsed on both flanks simultaneously — a template for assessing similar 'triangulation' strategies by other centrist incumbents facing populist-right and progressive-left pincer pressure.
insights.locator: Both these military and electoral messes are legacies of the same problem

insights.claim: Labour's rightward immigration policy did not peel off Reform UK voters; instead, the party's rhetoric about English flags and 'legitimate concerns' emboldened those voters' support for Reform UK or the breakaway Restore movement, while alienating left-leaning voters.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: Evidence that rightward triangulation on immigration backfires with the very voters it targets while costing votes on the left — a cautionary data point for centrist parties considering similar pivots elsewhere.
insights.locator: At the same time, Reform voters have proven to be largely unmoved

insights.claim: Labour is left with a fiscal hole of almost £5bn to fill in Andy Burnham's first budget, stemming from the unresolved Defence Investment Plan (DIP), which is almost certain to leave the Ministry of Defence short of its targets and further from the goal of spending 3.5 per cent of national income on defence by 2035.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: A concrete near-term fiscal constraint (~£5bn gap, 3.5% GDP defense target by 2035) for anyone modeling UK defense-sector spending, gilt issuance, or fiscal policy under the incoming Burnham government.
insights.locator: The outgoing prime minister has been unable to choose between guns and butter

insights.claim: Labour remains stuck at around 20 per cent of the intended vote share, lagging behind both Reform UK and the Conservatives, despite Starmer's 2024 election victory.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: A concrete polling benchmark for assessing UK political risk and the probability of a Reform UK-influenced or Reform-led government at the next general election.
insights.locator: Labour also remains trapped at around 20 per cent

insights.claim: Labour has focused its policy energy on appeasing voters who would never support the party (via a draconian immigration strategy) rather than its actual voter base, even though immigration numbers are now at their lowest level outside of Covid since 2012 without satisfying anti-immigration voters, who now demand deportations or 'remigration'; meanwhile the economy, not immigration, was the top issue for 2024 Labour voters ahead of the 2026 local elections.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: A misalignment-of-priorities thesis: governing parties chasing unwinnable voters on salient-but-wrong issues (immigration) while ignoring their base's actual priorities (economy) is a generalizable electoral-strategy failure mode to watch for in other democracies.
insights.locator: Perhaps the government’s biggest failing has been to misunderstand the electorate

insights.claim: Place-based regeneration spending in deprived 'left behind' areas has not translated into political gratitude: Hartlepool, England's highest per-head recipient of regeneration funding, voted almost uniformly for Reform UK, paralleling how the Welsh valley towns that received the most EU structural funding voted for Brexit.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: A recurring pattern — regeneration spending does not buy incumbent political loyalty in recipient areas — relevant to evaluating the political ROI of UK regional investment programs.
insights.locator: What about a sortie to deprived towns?

insights.claim: Morgan McSweeney stated in a recent interview that 'Preparation matters more than strategy… I'd like to tell you it's the other way around, but I think preparation counts more,' reflecting his short-term tactical electoral-strategist approach that the author argues succeeded narrowly in 2024 but left the party unprepared for governing choices.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: Characterizes the outgoing Labour leadership's operating philosophy as tactics-over-strategy, a framework for judging whether Andy Burnham's incoming team will repeat or correct that pattern.
insights.quote: Preparation matters more than strategy… I’d like to tell you it’s the other way around, but I think preparation counts more.
insights.locator: In a recent interview, Labour’s very own Clausewitz

insights.claim: Incoming Labour leader Andy Burnham, having won election as MP for Makerfield via a risky gambit, inherits both the fiscal defense hole and the party's electorate-misalignment problem, and will soon face major choices with 'precious little preparation,' risking the same kind of internal mutiny that ended Starmer's tenure.
insights.speaker: Ben Ansell
insights.so_what: Flags near-term political instability risk within the Labour government under Burnham, relevant to UK policy continuity and gilt/currency risk assessments tied to government stability.
insights.locator: But he cannot expect to rest on his laurels
